title: A Look at Space Hulk: Death Angel - The Card Game
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A5ojgoN39Lc

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns to Warhammer 40K with Space Hulk: Death Angel, Corey Konieczka's co-operative card game for Fantasy Flight. Its trick is capturing the white-knuckle tension of the Space Hulk board game — something he never quite feels playing that game solo — inside a tiny box, while adding enough of its own ideas to stand on its own. You arrange a battle line of Terminator marines, each with a facing, marching down corridors as gene stealer swarms spawn from terrain cards on either side.

The genius is in positioning and action selection: each colour of marine has support, move-and-activate, and attack cards, and once played a card is locked out until all are spent, so timing is everything. The dreaded d6 — only three skulls — means a 50% attack and instant death for any marine who rolls too low on defence. Support tokens grant precious re-rolls. Daniel notes the game is famously hard, references the 180-page strategy guide whose one genuinely useful page ranks squad colour-combos by difficulty (he plays the "cakewalk" red/purple/grey), and laments that it and its expansions are now wildly out of print. He'd love a generic reissue of this marching-line crawler.

----

Death Angel simulates a dungeon crawl with no board — just a battle line and facing. Do abstracted, board-less crawlers work for you, or do you need a map to move across?
